9 min read
Just now

--

Most coding advice was written for a version of this field that no longer exists.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most coding advice was written for a version of this field that no longer exists.
The advice hasn’t kept up.
Most of what gets passed around about learning to code — the roadmaps, the curriculum recommendations, the “here’s exactly what order to learn things in” threads — was written between 2015 and 2020 for a version of this field that has changed more in the last two years than it did in the previous decade. Following it now is a bit like getting directions to a city using a map from before a major highway was built. The fundamentals are still roughly right, but some of the most important turns are missing.
I learned this the hard way. I followed a curriculum someone had posted on Reddit in 2019 with genuine faith in the order, the tools, the platform recommendations. Most of it was fine. Some of it was subtly wrong in ways I didn’t figure out until I was deep enough in to feel the cost.
What I want to write here is the version of learning-to-code advice that accounts for where things actually are in 2025 — including the parts that feel uncomfortable to say out loud.

The Biggest Lie: “Just Follow the Roadmap”
There are roadmaps everywhere. Developer roadmaps, frontend roadmaps, backend roadmaps, full-stack roadmaps with so many branches they look like subway maps of major cities. They feel authoritative. They feel like if you just follow the sequence, competence will arrive at the end.
The problem is that a roadmap tells you what to learn but not how to learn it, and the how is where almost everyone actually fails. Specifically: roadmaps optimize for coverage, not retention. They’re built around the idea that if you’ve touched a topic, you can check it off and move forward. They have almost nothing to say about whether you’ve actually retained the ability to use what you touched, or whether you’ve built any intuition for applying it independently.
Cognitive scientist Robert Bjork’s research on “desirable difficulties” has been consistent for decades: the conditions that produce the strongest long-term learning are often the ones that feel most uncomfortable in the moment — retrieval practice rather than re-reading, spacing rather than massing, generating answers before being shown them. A roadmap that moves you linearly through topics with minimal repetition and maximum coverage is, from a learning science perspective, almost optimally designed to produce the illusion of learning without much of the actual thing.
Following the roadmap religiously is the wrong instinct. Using it as a rough map while doing the uncomfortable thing — trying to use concepts before you feel ready, building things that break, being confused for longer than feels okay — is closer to right.

What Changed in 2025 That Most Advice Ignores
I want to be specific here rather than vague, because “the landscape has changed” is a sentence anyone can write without saying anything.
Two concrete shifts matter.
First: the value of being able to write code from scratch, without external help, has changed relative to the value of being able to evaluate, debug, and understand code. This doesn’t mean writing from scratch is irrelevant — it very much isn’t — but the ratio has shifted. A developer who can critically evaluate AI-generated code, catch the plausible-but-wrong outputs, and make informed architectural decisions while using AI for the execution layer is more valuable now than a developer who can write everything from scratch but has no experience working alongside AI tools.
Most learning paths were built for the first model. The field currently rewards the second.
Second: the barrier to building real things has dropped dramatically, which changes the optimal learning strategy significantly. In 2018, a beginner who wanted to build a working web app needed to spend months on fundamentals before they could produce anything that ran in the real world. The cycle from learning to visible output was long, which made tutorials and structured courses a reasonable proxy for that output. In 2025, a beginner with basic technical literacy can build and deploy something functional within days, using AI assistance to fill gaps in their knowledge.
That shorter cycle changes the calculus. If you can build real things faster, you should build real things faster. The feedback from building something that actually runs — and breaks, and confuses you, and eventually works — is richer than the feedback from a tutorial exercise that was designed to succeed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Following along produces recognition. Building from nothing produces retention. They feel identical in the moment.
The Tutorial Problem (Again, But Differently)
I know I’m not the first person to say “tutorials are a trap,” and I’m aware that by the time an insight becomes the conventional wisdom, it’s usually been watered down into something that sounds right but doesn’t actually change behavior.
So let me be specific about what the tutorial trap looks like in practice, because the specific version of it matters.
You watch a tutorial. You follow along. The code works. You feel good. You close the laptop.
Two days later, you sit down to build something similar from scratch and you can’t remember any of it. Not because you weren’t paying attention — you were. But following along produces recognition, not retrieval. Your brain registered that the steps made sense, but never actually stored the ability to generate them independently.
The fix isn’t to take fewer tutorials. It’s to use tutorials differently. Watch a section, close the tutorial, try to reproduce what you just saw without looking. When you get stuck, check. Watch the next section, close the video, try again. The ratio of “watching” to “producing” should be roughly 1:3, which feels inefficient and is dramatically more effective.
I know this because I eventually started doing it out of frustration after my retention was clearly terrible, not because I’d thought it through. The frustration was the useful part.

The Thing You Actually Need to Build First
Not a to-do list app. I know that’s the conventional recommendation and I understand why — it’s simple, it touches most of the fundamental concepts, and it’s achievable early. But it’s also something nobody ever actually uses after they build it, which matters more than people acknowledge.
The motivational architecture of learning a skill that’s genuinely hard is fragile, especially in the early months when progress is slow and the gap between what you want to build and what you can build is embarrassingly wide. A project you actually care about — even slightly, even imperfectly — burns longer than one you built because someone’s curriculum told you to.
My first project I actually cared about was a tool that automated a specific annoying part of my previous job. Genuinely unglamorous. Not impressive to show anyone. But I actually wanted it to exist, so when I got stuck — which was constantly — the motivation to get unstuck came from somewhere real rather than from an abstract commitment to “learning to code.”
The closer your first project is to something you’d actually use, the better your chances of finishing it. A finished ugly thing is worth more, pedagogically, than an abandoned beautiful idea.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Using AI to learn faster is different from using AI to avoid learning. The output looks the same. The result doesn’t.
How to Use AI Tools Without Letting Them Use You
This is the section I want to write most carefully, because I think the current advice on this is genuinely confused in both directions.
Some learning resources say to avoid AI tools entirely while learning because they create dependence and prevent you from building real understanding. Some say to embrace them fully as the new normal. Both of these are too simple.
The honest answer is that it depends entirely on how you’re using them, and specifically on whether the AI is doing the thinking or supporting it.
Asking an AI “write me a function that does X” and copying the output is likely making your learning slower, not faster. Your brain didn’t engage with the problem, so it didn’t build any capacity for the next problem.
Asking an AI “explain why this approach causes this error” or “what would happen if I structured this differently” or “I’m trying to solve this — what concepts should I research?” — that’s using the tool in a way that adds information to your developing mental model rather than bypassing the need to build one.
The framing I’ve found most useful: use AI to learn faster, not to avoid learning. If an AI interaction ends with you understanding something you didn’t understand before, it worked. If it ends with working code you can’t explain, it didn’t — regardless of whether the code runs.

The Skills That Actually Transfer in 2025
Here’s where I want to be honest about some genuine uncertainty, because I don’t think anyone can predict with confidence exactly what the field looks like in three years.
What I’m more confident about: certain skills have become more valuable, not less, as AI tools become more central to the workflow. Debugging is the clearest example. Reading code you didn’t write, understanding what it does and why, and finding the specific place where it’s doing the wrong thing — this is more important now, not less, because more of the code in your environment was generated rather than authored by someone you can ask questions.
Systems thinking — the ability to understand how pieces fit together, what the downstream effects of a change are, where the failure points are — is similarly durable. AI can generate components. It’s genuinely weaker at reasoning about how components interact across a complex system, partly because it doesn’t have the context your specific codebase provides and partly because that kind of judgment is built from experience with things breaking in specific ways.
Communication is underrated in most programming curricula and increasingly central in actual practice. Writing a clear bug report, explaining a technical decision to a non-technical stakeholder, documenting why you made a choice rather than just what the choice was — these compound in value as your career develops in ways that any single technical skill doesn’t.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One small step that produces visible output. That’s the whole strategy.
What to Actually Do This Week
I’m going to resist the urge to give you a twelve-week plan, because twelve-week plans are exactly the kind of thing I just finished arguing against.
Instead: pick one thing you want to build that would be genuinely useful to you, even minimally. Not impressive. Useful. Describe it in a few sentences. Break it into the smallest first step that would produce any visible output at all. Try to do that step without a tutorial. When you get stuck, use whatever resource helps you get unstuck — including AI, including documentation, including a tutorial — but make sure you understand the solution before you move on.
That’s it. That’s the actual practice.
Everything else — the language debates, the platform comparisons, the roadmap arguments — is peripheral to that loop of try, get stuck, figure it out, try again. The developers I know who’ve learned this well all have different tech stacks, different learning histories, and different opinions about almost everything. They all did more or less that loop, more or less consistently, for long enough.
There isn’t a secret. The advice just usually makes it sound more complicated than it is, possibly because simple advice is harder to sell.

If you’ve been learning to code — or trying to start — I’d genuinely like to hear what’s been the actual sticking point. Not the generic version: the specific thing
